answer:
**In Urdu poetry, particularly in the ghazals and nazms of masters like Mirza Ghalib and Allama Iqbal, *dard* (درد) symbolizes the profound, bittersweet "pain" or "anguish" of love (*ishq*)—often romantic, spiritual, or existential longing associated with separation (*firaq*), unrequited passion, or divine yearning.**[^1][^2]

This *dard* is not mere physical ache but a cherished, almost sacred state: exquisite, intoxicating, and integral to the lover's (*aashiq*) identity. It is personified as a rare pearl or jewel too precious for the superficial world, preferred over healing (*dawa*), and celebrated for its depth amid a shallow, critic-ridden age. The lover delights in its "lazzat" (pleasure), as in "Ilaaj-e-dard mein bhi dard ki lazzat pe marta hoon" ("In treating anguish, I embrace its pain").[^3]

### Key Examples from the Corpus

#### Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, "Dard-e-Ishq" (The Anguish of Love)
The poem directly addresses *Dard-e-ishq* as a luminous pearl (*gohar-e-aab-daar*) hidden from the unworthy (*na-mehramon*). In a modern world of "science" and "critics" (*d aur-e-nukta-cheen*), it warns this pain to conceal itself:

> O, Anguish of Love! A pearl of purest water, you;  
> Be not revealed to the unworthy view.  
> Your sacred dwelling place lies veiled from sight,  
> This new assembly seeks but surface light.[^4]

Iqbal contrasts it with shallow spectacles: "Let not the urge for show be your desire, / Nor be indebted to the nightingale's sad choir." True *dard* thrives in secret solitude (*khalwat sara-e-raaz*), not public display.[^5]

#### Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, Ghazal 26
Ghalib epitomizes *dard*'s allure: the lover rejects cure to preserve its integrity.

> My pain would not be indebted to a cure;  
> I did not heal, nor did my state grow worse.[^6]

This ghazal rejects superficial remedies, valuing *dard* as authentic existence—"the free know grief for just a single breath"—over comfort.[^7]

### Broader Motif
Across Iqbal's *Bang-E-Dara* and Ghalib's *Diwan*, *dard* recurs in collocations like *dard-e-ishq*, *dard-e-firaaq*, or *dard-mandi*, evoking pleasure in suffering (*gham* philosophy: "A rose that has not lost a petal is no rose").[^8] It distinguishes true lovers from the masses, hidden like a "secret in some breast." The corpus (37 works, including Urdu poetry by Iqbal, Ghalib, Zauq) richly illustrates this, though not exhaustively all traditions.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17 "Dard-e-Ishq" ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/)).

[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 26 "Dard minnat-kash-e-dawa na hua" ([/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/](works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/)).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 2 ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/02-anokhi-waza-hai-saare-zamane-se-nirale-hain/translation/#p-4466e4](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/02-anokhi-waza-hai-saare-zamane-se-nirale-hain/translation/#p-4466e4)).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17, ¶ [p-6d19d8] ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-6d19d8](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-6d19d8)).

[^5]: Ibid., ¶ [p-31e939] ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-31e939](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-31e939)).

[^6]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 26, ¶ [p-fd05a7] ([/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/#p-fd05a7](works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/#p-fd05a7)).

[^7]: Ibid., ch. 51 ([/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/51-gham-nahin-hota-hai-aazaadon-ko-besh-az-yak-nafas/translation/](works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/51-gham-nahin-hota-hai-aazaadon-ko-besh-az-yak-nafas/translation/)).

[^8]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 7 "Falsafa-e-Gham" ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-141d8d](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-141d8d)).
